subject: literacy
title: Grade 6 Literacy
description: Argumentation, literary analysis, active vs passive voice, and formal writing.

lessons.title: Writing an Argument
lessons.objective: Learn to write a persuasive argument with evidence.
lessons.content:
An ARGUMENT essay tries to convince the reader of your viewpoint.

📐 STRUCTURE:
  1. CLAIM — state your opinion clearly
     'School uniforms should be compulsory.'

  2. REASONS — give 2-3 strong reasons
     'They create equality among students.'
     'They reduce morning decision fatigue.'
     'They promote school identity.'

  3. EVIDENCE — back up each reason
     'A study found that 73% of students feel less judged for clothes.'

  4. COUNTER-ARGUMENT — address the opposing view
     'Some argue uniforms limit self-expression, however...'

  5. CONCLUSION — restate your position strongly

🔑 PERSUASIVE TECHNIQUES:
  • Rhetorical questions: 'Wouldn't you want a fairer classroom?'
  • Statistics: 'Research shows that 8 out of 10 teachers agree.'
  • Emotive language: 'No child should feel excluded because of their clothes.'
  • Rule of three: lists of three are powerful and memorable

exercises.question: What is a 'counter-argument' in persuasive writing?
exercises.hint: Counter means 'against' — what might someone who disagrees say?
exercises.explanation: A counter-argument acknowledges what someone who disagrees might say, and then explains why your argument is still stronger.

exercises.question: Which of these is a rhetorical question?
options: How many students are in class?
Wouldn't you want to live in a clean world?
exercises.correctAnswer: Wouldn't you want to live in a clean world?
exercises.hint: A rhetorical question is asked for effect — you don't expect an actual answer.
exercises.explanation: A rhetorical question is asked to make a point, not to get an answer. 'Wouldn't you want to live in a clean world?' persuades the reader to agree.

lessons.title: Analysing Literature
lessons.objective: Learn how to analyse an author's choices and their effects.
lessons.content:
Literary analysis means looking at HOW and WHY an author writes the way they do.

🔍 KEY QUESTIONS:
  • What TECHNIQUES does the author use?
  • What EFFECT do they have on the reader?
  • WHY did the author make these choices?

📝 P.E.E. structure for analysis:
  POINT — make a point about the text
  EVIDENCE — quote from the text
  EXPLAIN — explain the effect on the reader

✏️ Example:
  Point: The author creates a sense of danger.
  Evidence: 'The shadows crept closer, swallowing the last light.'
  Explain: The personification of shadows 'creeping' and 'swallowing' makes them seem alive and threatening, creating tension for the reader.

🎨 Look for:
  • Word choice (why THAT word?)
  • Sentence length (short = tension, long = description)
  • Imagery (appeals to the senses)
  • Repetition (what's being emphasised?)

exercises.question: What does P.E.E. stand for in literary analysis?
exercises.hint: It's a structure for writing about texts: make your point, prove it, then say why it matters.
exercises.explanation: P.E.E. stands for Point (what you notice), Evidence (a quote from the text), and Explain (what effect it has on the reader).

exercises.question: Why might an author use short sentences in a tense scene?
options: Because they ran out of ideas
To create a fast, urgent pace
Because short is easier to write
exercises.correctAnswer: To create a fast, urgent pace
exercises.hint: Read these short sentences: 'He ran. The door locked. No escape.' — how does that feel?
exercises.explanation: Short sentences create pace and urgency. They make the reader read faster, building tension and excitement in action scenes.
